topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the shared apartment had become a physical weight on Maya’s chest. She kept glancing toward Leo’s side of the room, a knot tightening in her stomach with every minute she spent trying to focus on her astrophysics textbook. It was the way he’d been moving around lately—a strange, almost furtive energy. One Tuesday, she found it. Her journal, left carelessly open on her desk, had been closed again, but the pages were subtly shifted. A few lines, private thoughts about a difficult exam, were now underlined with a faint, almost imperceptible graphite smudge that wasn't hers. Maya’s jaw tightened. She didn't confront him immediately. Instead, she spent the next hour meticulously reorganizing her entire desk, stacking books with aggressive precision, a silent declaration that she expected things to return to their proper, untouched state. When Leo finally came in, whistling tunelessly, he didn't notice the shift in the atmosphere, only the slightly rearranged stationery.

[story 2]

"Hey, did you see my charger?" Liam asked, his voice annoyingly bright as he tossed his backpack onto the floor. He was sprawled on his bed, looking entirely too relaxed for someone who had just been studying for three straight days. Sarah watched him, her hands gripping the edge of her laptop so tightly her knuckles were white. She had been so certain she’d locked her diary in her drawer. But then she noticed the small, familiar leather-bound book sitting innocently on his nightstand, the clasp slightly ajar. A wave of hot frustration washed over her. She felt a sudden, sharp need to demand an explanation, to force him to acknowledge the violation of her space. Instead, she just stared at the book, her breathing growing shallow and quick. She decided to ignore it, to pretend the slight transgression hadn't happened, focusing instead on typing a furious, unrelated email, her fingers flying across the keyboard with unnecessary force.

[story 3]

Elias felt a slow burn starting behind his eyes. He’d been trying to maintain a façade of calm, a cool indifference that felt increasingly brittle. He’d noticed the pattern—the way his personal notes, scrawled in his worn notebook, seemed to be rearranged when he wasn't looking. It was subtle, almost artistic in its intrusion. One evening, he found his detailed observations on his philosophy course cross-referenced with personal anecdotes he’d only shared with no one. A deep, simmering resentment began to bubble up, making his palms sweat. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between his desk and the window, his movements jerky and agitated. He wanted to shout, to demand that someone respect the sanctity of his private thoughts, but the words caught in his throat. He just stood there, breathing heavily, his gaze fixed on the closed door of the room next to his, refusing to break eye contact with the wall.
